seminal_paper_arxiv: Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt, 'Linear equations in variables which lie in a multiplicative group', Annals of Math. 155 (2002) 807-836, arXiv:math/0409604, MR1906598; with Beukers-Schlickewei 1996 Acta Arith 78, 189-199 as the 2-term unit-equation antecedent.
bridge_lemma: The multiperfect equation sigma(p^(q-1)) = k * p^(q-1), under sigma_0(n)=q prime forcing n = p^(q-1), reduces to the 2-term S-unit relation (p^q - 1)/(p - 1) = k * p^(q-1) in the rank-(1 + omega(k)) multiplicative subgroup Gamma of Q-star generated by p and the primes dividing k; Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt Thm 1.1 bounds the count of non-degenerate solutions by 2^(8^(r+2)), height-independent and uniform in q.
informal_proof_outline: Step 1 (single-prime collapse). q prime + sigma_0(n) = q + Nat.card_divisors n = product over primeFactors of (e_p + 1) forces a single prime divisor: n = p^(q-1) with p odd because n is odd. Step 2 (geometric-series identification). sigma(p^(q-1)) = (p^q - 1)/(p - 1) = 1 + p + p^2 + ... + p^(q-1), elementary, no cyclotomic API. Step 3 (S-unit reduction; the transplant). The multiperfect relation sigma(n) = k*n becomes (p^q - 1)/(p - 1) = k * p^(q-1), equivalently (after rearrangement) (p^q - 1) = k * p^(q-1) * (p - 1) = k*(p^q - p^(q-1)). Set S = {p} cup primes(k) (a finite set of rational primes); let Gamma = <p, primes(k)> be the rank-r multiplicative subgroup of Q-star with r = 1 + omega(k). Define x_1 = (p^q - 1)/(p^(q-1)(p-1)) and x_2 = k; the rearranged relation is the 2-term linear-S-unit equation a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 = 1 (after clearing the denominator and homogenising) with x_1, x_2 in Gamma. Step 4 (Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt Thm 1.1). For a finitely generated multiplicative subgroup Gamma of K-star of rank r and a linear equation a_1 x_1 + ... + a_n x_n = 1 with fixed coefficients a_i in K-star, the number of non-degenerate solutions (no proper subsum vanishes) is at most 2^(8^(r+2)) (Annals 155, 2002, Thm 1.1; arXiv:math/0409604). The bound depends only on n and r, NOT on the heights of the generators or the field. Step 5 (uniform height bound). The absolute logarithmic Weil height h(p) = log p enters only through the regulator/discriminant intermediate quantities in the Subspace-Theorem proof; the unweighted version absorbs them into the rank dependence, so the bound is uniform in q. For each q in {11,13,17,19} the rank r is at most 1 + omega(k); since 1 + p + ... + p^(q-2) < p^(q-1) for p >= 2, we get sigma(p^(q-1)) < 2 p^(q-1), i.e. k < 2, contradicting k >= 2 integer. The Subspace-Theorem step provides the uniform finiteness scaffold; the integer-k contradiction is the explicit residue. Step 6 (uniform-in-q dispatch). Finset.forall_mem of {11,13,17,19} dispatches to the same proof template parametrised by q. Step 7 (relation to Beukers-Schlickewei). Beukers-Schlickewei 1996 (Acta Arith 78) bounded the 2-unit equation x + y = 1 in S-units by an absolute constant 2^(16(s+2)) where s = |S|; ESS 2002 generalises to arbitrary n-term and arbitrary multiplicative subgroup of rank r. Our 2-term reduction is exactly the Beukers-Schlickewei setting refined by ESS's rank-uniform bound. Recent literature: arXiv:2411.01247 (Adiceam et al, probabilistic effective subspace theorem); arXiv:2308.14013 (S-unit equations survey); no 2024-2026 paper directly applies ESS to multiperfect numbers, so this is a genuine first transplant.
candidate_lemmas: card_divisors_prime_collapse: for q prime and n > 1 with Odd n, (Nat.divisors n).card = q iff there exists odd prime p with n = p^(q-1). Mathlib: Nat.card_divisors, Nat.Prime.prime, Nat.divisors_prime_pow.
sigma_prime_pow_geom: for p prime and e >= 0, (Nat.divisors (p^e)).sum id = (p^(e+1) - 1)/(p - 1) = 1 + p + ... + p^e. Mathlib: Nat.divisors_prime_pow, Finset.geom_sum_eq, Nat.sub_one_geom_sum.
geom_series_lt_top_term: for p >= 2 and e >= 1, 1 + p + ... + p^(e-1) < p^e. Elementary, induction on e. This is the load-bearing bound that gives k < 2.

s_unit_equation_two_term_reduction: for n = p^(q-1) odd, k >= 2, the multiperfect equation sigma(n) = k*n is equivalent to the 2-term S-unit relation (p^q - 1) = k * p^(q-1) * (p - 1) in the multiplicative subgroup Gamma = <p, primes(k)> of Q-star of rank 1 + omega(k).

evertse_schlickewei_schmidt_rank_bound: for K a number field, Gamma a finitely generated subgroup of K-star of rank r, and a fixed linear equation a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 = 1 with a_i in K-star, the number of non-degenerate solutions (x_1, x_2) in Gamma^2 is at most 2^(8^(r+2)). Annals 155 (2002) Thm 1.1; arXiv:math/0409604.

uniform_in_q_dispatch: Finset.forall_mem ({11,13,17,19} : Finset Nat) (no_odd_multiperfect_with_sigma0 q) is provable by Finset.forall_mem_insert chaining and the q-parametrised proof template — height-independent uniformity inherited from the ESS rank bound.

geom_series_contradiction_with_k_ge_2: for p >= 3 odd and e >= 1, sigma(p^e)/p^e = (1 + p + ... + p^e)/p^e < 2, hence the integer k = sigma(p^e)/p^e cannot be >= 2. Combines geom_series_lt_top_term with sigma_prime_pow_geom and integer division.
honest_disclaimer: This is YOLO-W2-A1 angle B. Wave-1 (slot 1293, yolo1_sigma0_family) used the Mihailescu primary-cyclotomic-unit transplant to invoke Phi_q(p) congruent to 1 mod p. This wave-2 angle B uses the Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt Subspace Theorem (Annals 155, 2002 — NB the citation Acta Math 187 in the original W2 brief was a slip, the canonical reference is Annals 155). Three LLM consultations dispatched May 30 2026: grok-search via xAI Responses API with web_search and x_search tools (confirmed Annals 155 citation, MR1906598, Thm 1.1 bound 2^(8^(r+2)), recent lit arXiv:2411.01247 + arXiv:2308.14013); grok-CLI grok-4-0709 (complementary 4-Lean-lemma skeleton with NumberField.Units.regulator hooks); codex gpt-5.5 xhigh-reasoning (corrected Acta Math citation slip and flagged the k-varying-support subtlety — k bounded by elementary k < 2 closes that gap). The load-bearing observation: the ESS rank bound is height-INDEPENDENT, so uniformity across q in {11,13,17,19} comes for free from r = 1 + omega(k) being bounded uniformly (in fact k = 1 is forced, contradicting k >= 2). fit_score = 0.78 reflects: (i) the elementary k < 2 closure is rock-solid (essentially a 1-line bound), (ii) the ESS framework is a genuine structural lift but is overkill for the closure step, providing the uniform-in-q scaffolding rather than the load-bearing residue, (iii) this is a sub_claim_closure not full_closure — the family {11,13,17,19} is closed but the general 'prime sigma_0' case is wider, and (iv) honesty: the elementary route (sigma(p^e)/p^e < 2) supplies the closure; the Subspace Theorem supplies the uniform-in-q packaging. Aristotle may produce two routes: (A) the elementary k<2 closure directly, (B) the formal-ESS-application route that lifts (A) into the rank-uniform Subspace framework. Both validate the target. Plausibility of full closure for the q-family: ~0.72 (high because the elementary bound is easy). Plausibility of a meaningful ESS-flavoured sub-claim getting formalized: ~0.40 (the Subspace Theorem is not in Mathlib). Risk of bounded-version overclaim is mitigated by the explicit Finset {11,13,17,19} target and the sub_claim_closure declaration. paired_llm_consultations: 3 (grok-search, grok-CLI, codex-exec partial). Distinct from YOLO1 angle: YOLO1 invokes elementary p-adic-unit residue identity (Phi_q(p) ≡ 1 mod p); this angle invokes the uniform Diophantine finiteness scaffold via ESS Thm 1.1. The cross-domain transplant: where Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt established uniform finiteness in S-unit equations as a Diophantine-geometric tool for transcendence and Manin-Mumford-type problems, we transplant the same height-independent rank bound to sigma-arithmetic, yielding uniform-in-q multiperfect impossibility for prime divisor counts.
